original_abstract: This study investigates the intermolecular interactions within 17 organic acid–base complexes using a multi-technique approach combining X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), $^{15}$N solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (ssNMR) spectroscopy, and X-ray diffraction (XRD). By analyzing nitrogen centers acting as either Brønsted or hydrogen-bond acceptors, we demonstrate that N 1s binding energy shifts in XPS unequivocally distinguish between protonated species (salts) and hydrogen-bonded species (co-crystals). These findings are corroborated by significant low-frequency shifts in $^{15}$N ssNMR upon proton transfer. While co-crystal formation generally results in minor spectral changes, the formation of particularly strong hydrogen bonds can induce more pronounced shifts. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations using CASTEP were employed to predict isotropic $^{15}$N chemical shifts, showing excellent correlation with experimental data and facilitating the identification of structures via NMR crystallography. The consistent results across XPS, ssNMR, and computational modeling provide a robust methodology for characterizing local molecular environments. This integrated approach is particularly valuable for analyzing disordered or amorphous systems where traditional single-crystal XRD is not feasible, offering a generic framework for structural characterization in pharmaceutical and materials chemistry.
humanized_abstract: 17 organic acid–base complexes have been investigated with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), $^{15}$N solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (ssNMR) spectroscopy, and X-ray diffraction (XRD). In the solid-state, several nitrogen sites work as Brønsted or hydrogen-bond acceptors, and their detailed local structure can be disclosed by identifying protonated species as salts and hydrogen-bonded species as co-crystals by N 1s XPS, the large low-frequency $^{15}$N NMR shielding effect due to proton transfer, and minor changes except for a few large shifts due to very strong hydrogen bonds in NMR spectra of co-crystals. By combining the experimental NMR results with the Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations using CASTEP, excellent correlations between the calculated and experimental $^{15}$N isotropic chemical shifts are achieved and the structures are identified via NMR crystallography. Combining all these complementary results shows a powerful approach to disclose local structure and determine the state of proton or hydrogen bond in acid–base complexes. The approach is universal and especially for disordered or amorphous solids for which single-crystal XRD is not available. Hence, the structural information provided with this study has high relevance for pharmaceutical and materials chemistry.

By combining the experimental NMR results with the Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations using CASTEP, excellent correlations between the calculated and experimental $^{15}$N isotropic chemical shifts are achieved and the structures are identified via NMR crystallography. Combining all these complementary results shows a powerful approach to disclose local structure and determine the state of proton or hydrogen bond in acid–base complexes. The approach is universal and especially for disordered or amorphous solids for which single-crystal XRD is not available.
